vulnerability.title: Record Events that Modify User/Group Information - /etc/shadow
vulnerability.description: If the auditd daemon is configured to use the augenrules program to read audit rules during daemon startup (the default), add the following lines to a file with suffix .rules in the directory /etc/audit/rules.d: -w /etc/shadow -p wa -k audit_rules_usergroup_modification If the auditd daemon is configured to use the auditctl utility to read audit rules during daemon startup, add the following lines to /etc/audit/audit.rules: -w /etc/shadow -p wa -k audit_rules_usergroup_modification
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q audit && rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then # Perform the remediation for both possible tools: 'auditctl' and 'augenrules' # Create a list of audit *.rules files that should be inspected for presence and correctness # of a particular audit rule. The scheme is as follows: # # ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- # Tool used to load audit rules | Rule already defined | Audit rules file to inspect | # ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- # auditctl | Doesn't matter | /etc/audit/audit.rules | # ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- # augenrules | Yes | /etc/audit/rules.

triage_decision.reason: Adding an audit rule to monitor /etc/shadow modifications is a standard, low-risk configuration change that follows STIG hardening patterns and does not affect system functionality or access.

reasoning_messages.reasoning: We need to propose plan, then review. I'll do as required: FIRST message is the plan. Use review_plan, that will be responded. So my first message is the plan description.

review_feedback: The plan misses a critical step to reload or restart the auditd service after adding the rule. Without this, the new rule will not take effect until a reboot or manual reload, so the vulnerability remains unresolved.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan misses a critical step to reload or restart the auditd service after adding the rule. Without this, the new rule will not take effect until a reboot or manual reload, so the vulnerability remains unresolved.
concerns: Audit daemon does not automatically reload rules from /etc/audit/rules.d while running.
The plan assumes no restart is needed, which is incorrect on most platforms.
Could leave the system unmonitored if not reloaded.
No validation of rule presence after changes.
suggested_improvements: Include a command to reload rules, e.g., run '/usr/sbin/augenrules --load' or restart auditd with 'systemctl restart auditd' after modifying the file.
Verify the rule is active by checking output of 'auditctl -l' or 'augenrules -n' before and after changes.
Add idempotency checks for multiple occurrences of the same rule and ensure proper permissions on the created file (chmod 640, owned by root).
